author: @RWBHere
text: Best to wear some eye protection too. I've had bottles shatter during cutting. It's also important to keep the glass wet during grinding or polishing. Thanks for the video.

author: @statebased
text: Any risks of breathing the microwave air? Does the carbon based ink only include graphene, and no nanotubes?

replies.author: @smasimulationshop1028

author: @sofa-lofa4241
text:
GOGGLES!  Don't encourage people to break glass without wearing them yourself and instructing them to do so too,

A workshop microwave is fine for this but a kitchen one, no, the chance of tiny shards of glass stuck to your next ginsters pasty is not a great thought (may stop it tasting like cardboard though!)

Did you go from 80 grit straight to 1200 grit, or was it the 120 grit?  This was not clear

I love to see people having a go at stuff like this but please remember the safety aspect, if 50,000 people watch this then chances are 1 or 2 will get glass in their eye and 3 or 4 will be eating the glass stuck to their food.... There are a billion idiots out there and you shouldn't forget that

Sorry if this seems negative, I liked the content but the execution needed more thinking time

author: @paulwedlock9788
text:
Need's improvement!
1: prepare a length of masking tape to encompass the desired objective & then place the roll of tape on the bottle for cutting, as a guide for the tape or ink painting? If applicable.
2: shard's of glass fragments is not suitable for human ingestion!
Pacing said item in an old sock or paper towel's & then in a plastic bag: might reduce the contamination to a reasonably safe level? Depending on how ANGRY your mum gets :-/

author: @amazac2078
text: Luke nice video and great idea to use the ink as a cutting edge line for glass clever idea ,cheers mate!

author: @bashpr0mpt244
text: 1. This is not upcycling. 2. A bottle is useful and can store multiple things and be sealed. 3. A vase is useless and only holds flowers which you will never throw in the bin in time before they die so it's just going to look bad you should give those flowers to someone else instead. 4. You've just turned something useful into something useless. And you just gave away the flowers anyway so there's nothing to put in it.

replies.author: @keithfulkerson
replies.text: He said he's going to use it to hold pens.

replies.author: @-robo-
replies.text: Considering that most people just throw the bottle away, this is most certainly upcycling. Over the years, I have made tons of vases that are still in use today.  There are various methods of etching art or designs into the glass once it has been prepared. I also have made glasses, tumblers, votive dishes, lamps, luminaries, planters and a variety of other items. This gentleman's efforts have value and are completely above your petty scorn.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text:
Tbh, it's not useless it depends on what your wanting to do with said glass.
Right now i have a unique pen pot on my desk which holds all of my, paint brushes, pens ect. (otherwise I lose them all)
You know one mans trash is another treasure.
